source: a16z Podcast — Is there an AI bubble?
summary: No AI bubble today — GPU utilization vs. dark fiber is the key contrast to 2000
summary_extended: Baker argues that there is no AI bubble today, drawing a sharp contrast to the 2000 telecom bubble where 97% of fiber laid was dark (unused), whereas today every GPU is being utilized at or beyond capacity — sometimes to the point of melting during training runs. He supports this view with valuation data: Nvidia currently trades at approximately 40x trailing earnings versus Cisco's 150-180x at the peak of the 2000 bubble, suggesting far more reasonable pricing relative to the prior bubble analog. Further evidence comes from hyperscaler return on invested capital, which has increased roughly 10 percentage points since they began ramping AI capex, confirming that the spend is generating positive ROI rather than speculative waste. The investment implication is that AI infrastructure spending — particularly in GPUs and datacenter buildout — remains fundamentally justified, and that Nvidia specifically is not in bubble territory on a valuation basis relative to historical precedents.
detail: At the peak of the 2000 telecom bubble, 97% of fiber laid was dark (unused). Today there are no dark GPUs — all are being utilized at capacity, often to the point of melting during training runs. Nvidia trades at ~40x trailing earnings vs. Cisco's 150-180x at the 2000 peak. Hyperscalers' ROIC has increased ~10 points since ramping capex, confirming positive ROI on AI spend thus far.
confidence_evidence: Baker states 'I do not believe we're in an AI bubble today' and provides multiple quantitative comparisons; frames it as unambiguous rather than a debate

ticker_audit.reasoning: Nvidia is the central valuation subject of the thesis — Baker explicitly cites NVDA's ~40x trailing earnings as evidence it is not in bubble territory, making it a direct subject of the anti-bubble argument.

Cisco is the explicit historical analog used as the bubble benchmark (150-180x earnings at the 2000 peak), making it a direct subject of the comparative valuation argument even though it is the negative example.

As a hyperscaler whose ROIC has increased ~10 points since ramping AI capex, Google is a direct beneficiary of the thesis that AI spend is generating positive ROI — Baker's hyperscaler ROIC point directly validates their capital allocation.
